---
title: "Java not included in restricted extras"
date: 2012-07-26
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by Nightmist on 2012-07-26
Hello,

I've installed the restricted extras, and in the description it says that java is included. But as far as I can tell, this isn't the case. So... did something fail to install on my system, or is the description erroneous?

---

### Post by Bufeu on 2012-07-26
What does the log (**var/log/dpkg.log**) says?

EDIT: What browser do you use? What happens if you click on "[Verify Java version]("http://java.com/en/download/installed.jsp")"?

---

I use Chromium. I had to install java seperately (OpenJDK Java 7 Runtime), but still it doesn't work: "No plug-in available to display this content."

When I run the command "java -version" command, it returns:

java version "1.7.0_03"
OpenJDK Runtime Environment (IcedTea7 2.1.1pre) (7~u3-2.1.1~pre1-1ubuntu3)
OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM (build 22.0-b10, mixed mode)

### Post by QIII on 2012-07-26
Are you looking for OpenJDK 7 or Oracle Java 7?  Use OpenJDK 7 if you can, since it is the open source reference implementation of Oracle Java 7.  However, because much of the world does not recognize OpenJDK, Oracle Java may be necessary.

Oracle Java itself is not included because Oracle has withdrawn all licensing for anyone, including Microsoft, to have it in their repositories.

What is in the repo is an installer from webupd8.org that scripts the download and installation from Oracle.

Either try

```
sudo apt-get install oracle-java7-installer
```or look in the Oracle Java 7 section of the wiki in my signature for a link with the text "Using webupd8.org's strikingly simple method".

In either case, you may need to find out where Chromium expects the plugin to be and copy it there, if the FF plugin will even work.

---

### Post by directhex on 2012-07-26
> **Nightmist said:**
> Hello,

I've installed the restricted extras, and in the description it says that java is included. But as far as I can tell, this isn't the case. So... did something fail to install on my system, or is the description erroneous?

It used to be.

Oracle revoked the license allowing closed-source Oracle Java to be included in distributions, so it isn't anymore.

Someone may have forgotten to update the package description to reflect it.

### Post by QIII on 2012-07-27
Because Oracle wants control of their intellectual property.

It can easily be installed in Windows or Linux by visiting their site.

For Ubuntu, webupd8.org has automated the process so updates can come through normal periodic updates as you do them -- and not have to reinstall each time they release a new update.

### Post by ntzrmtthihu777 on 2012-11-27
[FONT=Courier New]Also, if installing java is a bit tough on you (it killed me) there is a very nice script [here](https://github.com/flexiondotorg/oab-java6) that creates a local apt-repo on your machine, allowing an easy installation with
[/FONT]```
[FONT=Courier New]sudo apt-get install sun-java6-jre[/FONT]
```[FONT=Courier New]
or
[/FONT]```
[FONT=Courier New]sudo apt-get install oracle-java7-jre[/FONT]
```
[FONT=Courier New](the script allows you to create the apt-repo for 6 or 7)
Slight issue with 6, you have to manually download the *bin for both 32x and 64x and place them in the correct dir (been a while since I did it, check the issues page in the link) and run the script again, but all in all its a pretty sweet setup.
[/FONT][FONT=Courier New][/FONT]
